bibliographic_data.title: Warping the Edge: Where Instant Mobility in 5G Meets Stateful Applications
bibliographic_data.abstract: Edge computing is considered a key paradigm for supporting real-time applications over 5G networks, as hosting applications at the network edge can substantially reduce delays. However, one challenge with hosting mobile applications on the network edge is ensuring that users continue to get low latency as they move across different locations. This requires support to handover clients to different edge sites with negligible application delays. Many of these edge apps are stateful and migrating stateful apps over 5G can cause significant app downtime during mobility. In this paper, we first identify the key architectural issues in enabling fast app handovers during mobility and then propose a new system design, EdgeWarp, that mitigates delays through proactive application state migration.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper proposes EdgeWarp, a cross-layer system design for enabling fast and reliable handovers of stateful applications over 5G networks through proactive application state migration.
core_content.key_methodology: EdgeWarp uses a multi-step early target BS prediction pipeline that leverages Radio Network Information (RNI) APIs in MEC to predict handovers in advance using an LSTM network, then extrapolates radio measurements to enable early target BS prediction, and finally performs the prediction. This allows edge apps to initiate app state migration proactively.
outcomes: Up to a 15.4× reduction in application downtime under mobility
Proactive application state migration through early target BS prediction
core_content.full_text_markdown:
Edge computing is considered a key paradigm for supporting real-time applications over 5G networks, as hosting applications at the network edge can substantially reduce delays. However, one challenge with hosting mobile applications on the network edge is ensuring that users continue to get low latency as they move across different locations. This requires support to handover clients to different edge sites with negligible application delays. Many of these edge apps are stateful and migrating stateful apps over 5G can cause significant app downtime during mobility. In this paper, we first identify the key architectural issues in enabling fast app handovers during mobility and then propose a new system design, EdgeWarp, that mitigates delays through proactive application state migration.

The paper identifies three key issues: uncoordinated and reactive app session migration, slow app session migration, and 5G handover. To address these, the authors propose EdgeWarp, which uses early target BS prediction to enable proactive application state migration. The system design includes a multi-step pipeline that leverages Radio Network Information (RNI) APIs in MEC to predict handovers using an LSTM network, then extrapolates radio measurements for early target BS prediction.

The evaluation shows up to a 15.4× reduction in application downtime under mobility.
